comments.body: As a general rule of thumb, you shouldn't use the == and != operators on anything but primitive types. See Oli's and Matti's answers for an explanation.
simple test: compare new String("abc") to "abc" : "abc"!= new String("abc") should result in true; ! "abc".equals(new String("abc")) is false

answers.body:
In general, no they're not the same. The first version checks whether elem1 and elem2 are references to the same object (assuming that they're not primitive types). The second version calls a type-specific method to check whether two (possibly distinct) ojects are "equal", in some sense (often, this is just a check that all their member fields are identical).

I don't think this has anything to do with generics, as such.

== (and by extension !=) check for object identity, that is, if both of the objects refer to the very same instance. equals checks for a higher level concept of identity, usually whether the "values" of the objects are equal. What this means is up to whoever implemented equals on that particular object. Therefore they are not the same thing.

A common example where these two are not the same thing are strings, where two different instances might have the same content (the same string of characters), in which case a == comparison is false but equals returns true.

The default implementation of equals (on Object) uses == inside, so the results will be same for objects that do not override equals (excluding nulls, of course)

comments.body: note: the here called "class types" or "classes" is officially called reference types (JLS 4.1)
@user85421 Thanks. I will add that fact and link.

answers.body:
Parallel type systems

You must understand that Java has two separate type systems: primitives (byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean) and references (classes & objects).

See Java Language Specification for technical details.

You can use == & != with both types, but only reference types have an equals method.

  • For primitive types, testing for equality with == means asking if two variables hold the same data, the same number or the same Boolean.
  • For reference types in Java, a variable does not actually hold an object. A reference variable always holds a reference that leads you elsewhere to the object. Think of a reference as basically a pointer to the location in memory where the object lives. Testing for equality with == effectively asks if two variables hold the same pointer, the same address location in memory. Meaning, do the two variables lead to the very same object? Testing for equality with == does not ask if two objects contain the same data values in their member fields.

Payload

In other words, for primitive types, the == & != compares the payload you care about. For reference types, the == & != compare does not compare the payload you care about, does not compare content of the object.

Primitive type Reference type
== & != compares value of the data == & != compares value of the reference pointers, asking if they both represent the same address in memory, leading to the very same object.
== & != compares payload == & != does not compare payload.

To compare the payload that you care about within two objects, you may have any of these three avenues:

  • Call equals if the class is documented as overriding the default inherited Object#equals method.
  • Call compareTo if the class implements the Comparable interface.
  • Call upon an object of a Comparator class written to semantically compare objects of your specific class.

Every class in Java inherits from Object class. So every object inherits the equals class from Object. By default, that method acts the same as ==, comparing the reference pointer memory location. To instead compare the content of the objects, the class must override the equals method to provide a different implementation that examines the payload content of the member fields.

Example

As an example, consider the primitive type int, and the wrapper class Integer.

// Primitive type
int x = 42 , y = 42 ;
boolean same = ( x == y ) ;  // Will always be `true`.

The Integer class is documented as overriding the inherited Object#equals method to compare the value of the contained integer number.

Java may or may not choose, for efficiency, to create only a single object, assigning one object to both reference variables. So we may have two separate objects at two different memory addresses, each holding forty-two inside. Or, we may have only one object at a single memory address.

// Reference type
Integer x = Integer.valueOf( 42 ) , y = Integer.valueOf( 42 ) ; // 
boolean sameObject = ( x == y ) ;  // Might be `true`, might be `false`.
boolean samePayload = x.equals( y ) ;  // Will always be `true`.

As a convenience, Java can automatically convert the primitive literal 42 to an object of the wrapper class Integer. This process is called auto-boxing. So we can rewrite the code above like this:

// Reference type
Integer x = 42 , y = 42 ; // Autoboxing converts the primitive literal `42` to an object of the wrapper class `Integer`. 
boolean sameObject = ( x == y ) ;  // Might be `true`, might be `false`.
boolean samePayload = x.equals( y ) ;  // Will always be `true`.

Could result in this diagram, if Java chose to recycle an object for efficiency:

In that case, x == y is true, both references point to the very same object.

Or could result in two separate objects, each referenced separately as seen in this diagram:

In that case, x == y is false, each reference points to separate objects located at different locations in memory.

The code Integer y = x ; would definitely result in the upper diagram, two references pointing to the same object.

Records

Note that classes defined as a record by default implicitly override Object#equals, comparing each and every field.

Person ( String name , LocalDate birthDate ) {}
Person alice = new Person( "Alice" , LocalDate.of( 1984 , Month.JANUARY , 23 ) ;
Person alice2 = new Person( "Alice" , LocalDate.of( 1984 , Month.JANUARY , 23 ) ;
boolean samePayload = alice.equals( alice2 ) ;  // Always true (given that here we rely on default implicit override of `Object#equals`).

hashCode

Reminder: If you write a class overriding Object#equals, you likely should also override Object#hashCode with the same semantics. Search to learn more.

Not really confusing

If you are new to programming, this may seem confusing, even overwhelming. Just give it some time, and practice. General rule as a newbie: Avoid using == with objects.

In day-to-day programming work, we navigate these two type systems without really thinking about it. Only in unusual corner-cases do we become consciously aware.

Also, the Java team at Oracle is working on future changes to bridge the two type systems. But that future is years away.

title: Difference between the two Java operators: != vs !equals
body:
Is this code:

elem1!=elem2

equivalent to this one?

!elem1.equals(elem2)

It compiles both ways, but I'm still unsure about it...
